markdownRemark.html:
As I work on small projects in Bevy, I like to share what I’ve learned. This is going to be a small tutorial but it’ll cover the essentials of loading a spritesheet, watching for user input, and moving the character in response.

Getting Started

To get started, we need to create a new project. I just named it bevy_simple_spritesheet but you can name it whatever you wish.

cargo new bevy_simple_spritesheet

Now in the generated Cargo.toml file, we need to add our dependencies. Since this is a simple example, we’ll just need Bevy. What’s also exciting is that this is our first project using v0.6.0.

[dependencies]
bevy = "0.6.0"

Define the Game States

One of the first things I always like to do with a new project is define our game’s states. While this simple example will only use one state, it can be expanded on later.

Create the src/state.rs file and add our single state:

/// The possible states that the game can be in.
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, Hash, PartialEq)]
pub enum GameState {
    // The main state of the game where the user is playing.
    Game,
}

Bevy Initial Setup

Now that we’ve added Bevy as our dependency, let’s create the basic structure of the app by defining the 2D camera and our starting state in the src/main.rs file.

use bevy::prelude::*;

mod state;

use state::*;

fn main() {
    App::new()
        // Add Bevy's default plugins.
        .add_plugins(DefaultPlugins)
        // Add the initial game state.
        .add_state(GameState::Game)
        // Adds the 2D camera.
        .add_startup_system(spawn_camera_system)
        // Run the game!
        .run();
}

/// Spawns the 2D camera of the game.
/// 
/// `commands` - Used to spawn the camera.
fn spawn_camera_system(mut commands: Commands) {
    commands.spawn_bundle(OrthographicCameraBundle::new_2d());
}

This is pretty simple, we didn’t even define the window as that’s not a big part of this tutorial. If you run this it won’t do much so we’ll define our player next.

The first thing we’ll do is spawn the player. One thing we’ll need to do is get a spritesheet to use for the player. I used the Adventurer-v1.5-Sheet. This spritesheet has a lot of animations we won’t use but

frontmatter.title: Creating a Simple Spritesheet Animation with Input
frontmatter.description: Learn how to animate a spritesheet when the user presses the movement keys
